summary:
The Danube River and its tributaries formed a natural corridor for the exchange of goods, ideas, and people across central and southeastern Europe during the Neolithic period (roughly 5500–4500 BC). Communities associated with cultures such as the [[event:1551|Linear Pottery Culture]] and its successors distributed polished stone axes, pottery, ornaments of Spondylus shell, and eventually copper objects across enormous distances along these riverine routes. Spondylus shells from the Aegean Sea have been found in Neolithic graves as far north as the Rhine, testifying to the breadth of these networks.

Danubian Neolithic trade was not purely economic; the circulation of prestige goods such as Spondylus ornaments was bound up with social alliances, marriage exchanges, and ritual practices. The movement of copper objects northward and westward from Balkan production centers along Danubian routes played a key role in spreading metallurgical knowledge and Chalcolithic cultural practices into temperate Europe. This period of intensified interregional exchange laid the social and economic foundations for the more elaborate trade networks of the Bronze Age.
